instructions:
You are an expert software engineer debugging C/C++ programs. You
interact with a Unix shell via two tools — `bash` for general shell
commands and `gdb` for stateful debugger commands against the buggy
binary.

queries.user_text:
You're debugging a C/C++ bug. The buggy binary is at `./build/prog` and the source file is `program.c` in the current directory.

Run command: `./build/prog`

Expected behavior: crashes when run (likely a sanitizer report or signal).

You have a stateful gdb session pre-loaded with `./build/prog` (argv already configured). Use `gdb` for runtime debugging, `bash` for source inspection. Identify the root cause and propose both a local fix and a structural global fix.
queries.prompt:
You're debugging a C/C++ bug. The buggy binary is at `./build/prog` and the source file is `program.c` in the current directory.

Run command: `./build/prog`

Expected behavior: crashes when run (likely a sanitizer report or signal).

You have a stateful gdb session pre-loaded with `./build/prog` (argv already configured). Use `gdb` for runtime debugging, `bash` for source inspection. Identify the root cause and propose both a local fix and a structural global fix.
queries.response:
ROOT CAUSE: program.c:11 has an off-by-one error in the for loop condition (i <= len instead of i < len), causing an out-of-bounds read from buf[len] at program.c:12 when i == len.
LOCAL FIX: Change the loop condition from "i <= len" to "i < len".
GLOBAL FIX: Introduce runtime bounds checking (e.g., via assertions like assert(i < len) inside the loop) or refactor to use safer abstractions like a struct with buffer and length, combined with compile-time checks using AddressSanitizer in development builds to prevent this class of buffer overflow bugs.

tool_calls.call:
break program.c:12
run
info locals
p i
p len
p buf[i]

delete breakpoints
break program.c:11
run
info locals
p buf
